title: DeltaProduct: Increasing the Expressivity ofDeltaNet Through Products of Householders
authors: Julien Siems‚ąó‚ôĘ,¬†¬†Timur Carstensen‚ôĘ‚ô£,¬†¬†Arber Zela‚ôĘ,Frank Hutter‚ôĘ‚ô£,¬†¬†Massimiliano Pontil‚ô°‚ô†, Riccardo Grazzi‚ąó‚ėÖ\hskip 3.98337pt{}^{\bigstar}Equal contribution‚ąó, CSML, Istituto Italiano di Tecnologia‚ô°, University of Freiburg‚ôĘ,ELLIS Institute T√ľbingen‚ô£, AI Centre, University College London‚ô†, Microsoft Research‚ėÖjuliensiems@gmail.comriccardograzzi4@gmail.comWork started while at Istituto Italiano di Tecnologia.
abstract: Linear Recurrent Neural Networks (linear RNNs) have emerged as competitive alternatives to Transformers for sequence modeling, offering efficient training and linear-time inference. However, existing architectures face a fundamental trade-off between expressivity and efficiency, dictated by the structure of their state-transition matrices. While diagonal matrices used in architectures like Mamba, GLA, or mLSTM yield fast runtime, they suffer from severely limited expressivity. To address this, recent architectures such as (Gated) DeltaNet and RWKVv7 adopted a diagonal plus rank-1 structure, allowing simultaneous token-channel mixing, which overcomes some expressivity limitations with only a slight decrease in training efficiency. Building on the interpretation of DeltaNet‚Äôs recurrence as performing one step of online gradient descent per token on an associative recall loss, we introduce DeltaProduct, which instead takes multiple (nhn_{h}) steps per token. This naturally leads to diagonal plus rank-nhn_{h} state-transition matrices, formed as products of nhn_{h} generalized Householder transformations, providing a tunable mechanism to balance expressivity and efficiency and a stable recurrence. Through extensive experiments, we demonstrate that DeltaProduct achieves superior state-tracking and language modeling capabilities while exhibiting significantly improved length extrapolation compared to DeltaNet. Additionally, we also strengthen the theoretical foundation of DeltaNet‚Äôs expressivity by proving that it can solve dihedral group word problems in just two layers.

paragraphs: The Transformer architecture (Vaswani et al., 2017) has revolutionized natural language processing through its self-attention mechanism, enabling both parallel computation across sequence length and effective context retrieval. Despite outperforming traditional LSTM models (Hochreiter & Schmidhuber, 1997) across numerous tasks, Transformers‚Äô quadratic computational complexity with sequence length presents challenges when dealing with longer sequences. Linear RNNs have emerged as a promising solution that combines parallel training across sequence length with linear inference-time complexity. At the core of these models are the state-transition matrices governing the recurrence, which fundamentally determine their expressivity (Merrill et al., 2024). While early linear RNNs like S4 (Gu et al., 2022) or LRU (Orvieto et al., 2023) use token-independent state-transition matrices, current linear RNNs exclusively use token-dependent state-transition matrices due to their superior expressivity. The first generation of token-dependent linear RNNs, including Mamba (Gu & Dao, 2024; Dao & Gu, 2024), GLA (Yang et al., 2024a), and mLSTM (Beck et al., 2024), uses diagonal state-transition matrices for efficient sequence processing. More recent architectures have incorporated non-diagonal structures, often diagonal plus rank-1, enabling simultaneous mixing of information across both tokens and channels. This innovation has led to more expressive models such as (Gated) DeltaNet (Yang et al., 2024b; 2025), TTT-Linear (Sun et al., 2024), RWKV-7, and Titans (Behrouz et al., 2024), which demonstrate superior language modeling and in-context retrieval performance, with only a slight decrease in training efficiency.

Recent work has revealed a fundamental trade-off between training efficiency and expressivity of linear RNNs, dictated by the structure of their state-transition matrices (Merrill et al., 2024; Sarrof et al., 2024; Grazzi et al., 2025). Models with diagonal state-transition matrices, such as Mamba and GLA, are highly efficient to train but face severe expressivity limitations - for instance, they cannot perform addition modulo 3 on arbitrary length sequences in finite precision (Grazzi et al., 2025, Theorem 2). Also Transformers face similar limitations (Hahn, 2020; Merrill & Sabharwal, 2023), since they can be seen as special linear RNNs with state-transition matrix equal to the identity, albeit with an infinite dimensional state (Katharopoulos et al., 2020). DeltaNet partially overcomes these limitations through generalized Householder matrices, achieving greater expressivity with only a modest increase in training cost, though it still requires multiple layers for certain tasks. At the other extreme, linear RNNs with full state-transition matrices offer maximal expressivity (Cirone et al., 2024), capable of recognizing any regular language with a single layer (Merrill et al., 2024), but are prohibitively expensive to train.

To bridge this gap, we propose DeltaProduct, a method that balances expressivity and efficiency of the recurrence computation. While DeltaNet‚Äôs recurrence performs a single gradient step per token on the squared loss of a linear key-to-value mapping (Wang et al., 2025; Yang et al., 2024b), DeltaProduct takes nhn_{h} gradient steps using additional keys and values, yielding state-transition matrices that are products of nhn_{h} generalized Householder matrices. This connection between the number of optimization steps and the matrix structure provides an elegant way to interpolate between diagonal and dense matrices: increasing the number of gradient steps automatically increases the number of Householder matrices in the product, providing a tunable mechanism to control the recurrence‚Äôs expressivity. Additionally, this product enables precise control over the norm of state transition matrices, ensuring they remain ‚Č§1\leq 1 to maintain stability during training on long sequences.
Concretely, we make the following contributions:
‚ÄĘ We propose DeltaProduct, which generalizes DeltaNet by using products of Householders as state-transition matrices (Section 3).

‚ÄĘ We prove that DeltaNet with 2 layers and an expanded eigenvalue range can solve word problems for dihedral groups (including S3S_{3}), extending prior analysis that was limited to cyclic groups (Grazzi et al., 2025, Theorem 6). This advances our understanding of DeltaNet‚Äôs expressivity when multiple layers are used (Section 4).

‚ÄĘ We empirically validate DeltaProduct‚Äôs superior performance across multiple domains: solving complex state-tracking tasks beyond DeltaNet‚Äôs capabilities (see Figure 1), achieving better results on Chomsky hierarchy benchmarks, and improving language modeling performance with significantly enhanced length extrapolation (Section 5).

subsections.title: 2.1 Linear RNNs
paragraphs: Linear RNNs consist of stacked layers, each processing an input sequence of vectors ūĚíô1,‚Ä¶,ūĚíôt‚ąą‚ĄĚl{\bm{x}}_{1},\dots,{\bm{x}}_{t}\in\mathbb{R}^{l} to produce an output sequence of vectors ūĚíö^1,‚Ä¶,ūĚíö^t‚ąą‚ĄĚp\hat{{\bm{y}}}_{1},\dots,\hat{{\bm{y}}}_{t}\in\mathbb{R}^{p}. We write the forward pass of each layer placing emphasis on the linear recurrence (as in Grazzi et al. (2025)) as

ūĚĎĮ0‚ąą‚ĄĚn√ód{\bm{H}}_{0}\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times d} is the initial hidden state, ūĚĎ®:‚ĄĚl‚Üí‚ĄĚn√ón{\bm{A}}:\mathbb{R}^{l}\to\mathbb{R}^{n\times n} maps the input to a state-transition matrix, ūĚĎ©:‚ĄĚl‚Üí‚ĄĚn√ód{\bm{B}}:\mathbb{R}^{l}\to\mathbb{R}^{n\times d}, and dec:‚ĄĚn√ód√ó‚ĄĚl‚Üí‚ĄĚp\mathrm{dec}:\mathbb{R}^{n\times d}\times\mathbb{R}^{l}\to\mathbb{R}^{p}. The functions ūĚĎ®{\bm{A}}, ūĚĎ©{\bm{B}}, and dec\mathrm{dec} are learnable, with dec\mathrm{dec} typically containing a feedforward neural network. Different linear RNN variants are distinguished by their specific implementations of these functions. For example, Mamba (Gu & Dao, 2024; Dao & Gu, 2024), GLA (Yang et al., 2024a), and mLSTM (Beck et al., 2024) use variations of a diagonal state-transition matrix ūĚĎ®‚Äč(ūĚíôi){\bm{A}}({\bm{x}}_{i}). For a comparison of different linear RNN architectures see Yang et al. (2024b, Table 4). The linearity of the recurrence allows it to be parallelized along the sequence length, either via a chunkwise parallel form (Hua et al., 2022; Sun et al., 2023; Yang et al., 2025) or using a parallel scan (Blelloch, 1990; Martin & Cundy, 2018; Smith et al., 2023; Fan et al., 2024; Gu & Dao, 2024).

We base our work on the DeltaNet architecture (Schlag et al., 2021a; b), which has recently attracted renewed interest through the work of Yang et al. (2024b; 2025). The DeltaNet layer is parameterized as

where ő≤t=sigmoid‚Äč(ūĚíėő≤‚ä§‚ÄčūĚíôt)\beta_{t}=\mathrm{sigmoid}({\bm{w}}_{\beta}^{\top}{\bm{x}}_{t}), ūĚíít,ūĚíĆt‚ąą‚ĄĚn{\bm{q}}_{t},{\bm{k}}_{t}\in\mathbb{R}^{n} (with ‚ą•ūĚíĆt‚ą•=1\lVert{\bm{k}}_{t}\rVert=1), ūĚíót‚ąą‚ĄĚd{\bm{v}}_{t}\in\mathbb{R}^{d} are output of learnable functions of ūĚíôt{\bm{x}}_{t} and ūĚíėő≤‚ąą‚ĄĚl{\bm{w}}_{\beta}\in\mathbb{R}^{l} is a learnable parameter. DeltaNet‚Äôs state-transition matrices are generalized Householder transformations. Unlike diagonal matrices which only mix tokens, these non-diagonal transformations enable token-channel mixings, significantly enhancing the model‚Äôs expressivity compared to diagonal linear RNNs (Grazzi et al., 2025; Merrill et al., 2024). From a geometric perspective, the parameter ő≤t\beta_{t} controls the type of transformation: ő≤t=0\beta_{t}=0 corresponds to the identity transformation, ő≤t=1\beta_{t}=1 yields a projection operation, and ő≤t=2\beta_{t}=2 produces a reflection in the hyperplane defined by ūĚíĆt{\bm{k}}_{t}. DeltaNet also has a natural interpretation from an online learning perspective. As noted by Yang et al. (2024b); Wang et al. (2025); Liu et al. (2024), each step of the DeltaNet recurrence can also be viewed as one step of online gradient descent on a quadratic loss:

Recent work by Grazzi et al. (2025) demonstrates that expanding the eigenvalue range of linear RNNs‚Äô state transition matrices from [0,1][0,1] to [‚ąí1,1][-1,1] significantly enhances their expressivity. For DeltaNet, this modification requires only a simple scaling of ő≤t\beta_{t} by 2, enabling the network to handle state-tracking tasks such as parity checking and, more generally, any group word problem where each element of the input sequence corresponds to a permutation of at most two elements. A group word problem associated with a group GG consists in mapping sequences of group elements x1,‚Ä¶,xtx_{1},\dots,x_{t} with xi‚ąąGx_{i}\in G into sequences y1,‚Ä¶,yty_{1},\dots,y_{t}, where yi=x1‚čÖx2‚Äč‚čĮ‚Äčxiy_{i}=x_{1}\cdot x_{2}\cdots x_{i} and ‚čÖ\cdot is the group operation. The group word problem of the permutation group of 5 elements (S5S_{5}) is notoriously hard to solve for both Transformers and linear RNNs (Liu et al., 2023; Merrill & Sabharwal, 2023; Merrill et al., 2024).

subsections.title: 2.2 Related Work
paragraphs: Linear RNNs have recently been approached from two main perspectives: state-space models and causal linear attention. State-space models, originally developed for continuous dynamical systems, inspired several variants including S4 (Gu et al., 2022), H4 (Fu et al., 2021), and LRU (Orvieto et al., 2023) (see Tiezzi et al. (2024) for a comprehensive survey). Recent works like Mamba (Gu & Dao, 2024; Dao & Gu, 2024) enhance these models by incorporating input-dependent gating to the hidden state, leading to significant improvements in language modeling performance. In a parallel line of research, Katharopoulos et al. (2020) demonstrated that causal linear attention Transformers can be reformulated as RNNs with linear scaling in sequence length. Following this insight, Yang et al. (2024a) developed Gated Linear Attention (GLA), which incorporates a gating mechanism similar to Mamba. Several recent approaches have explored more expressive recurrence patterns through non-diagonal state-transition matrices, including DeltaNet (Schlag et al., 2021a; Irie et al., 2023; Yang et al., 2024b), TTT-Linear (Sun et al., 2024), RWKV7, and Titans (Behrouz et al., 2024). Additionally, Beck et al. (2024) introduced xLSTM, an advanced version of LSTM (Hochreiter & Schmidhuber, 1997) that combines non-linear and linear RNN architectures. Our work shares conceptual similarities with Adaptive Computation Time (ACT) (Graves, 2016), as both approaches allow RNNs to dynamically determine the computational steps required for each input, resulting in enhanced flexibility and task performance. This adaptive approach has been further developed in works like the Universal Transformer (Dehghani et al., 2019), with recent work by Geiping et al. (2025) demonstrating its effectiveness in modern reasoning tasks.

In this work, we propose DeltaProduct, a generalization of DeltaNet that enhances its expressivity by featuring a state transition matrix formed as a product of generalized Householder matrices. While DeltaNet‚Äôs recurrence can be seen as performing one step of online gradient descent per token, DeltaProduct further refines the hidden state update by optimizing the objective multiple times per token, naturally leading to a more expressive state-transition matrix, where each additional step expands the range of achievable linear transformations.

Formally, for each input token xix_{i} to the layer we generate nhn_{h} keys as ūĚíĆi,j{\bm{k}}_{i,j} = ūĚĎĺj‚ÄčūĚíôi/‚ą•ūĚĎĺj‚ÄčūĚíôi‚ą•2{\bm{W}}_{j}{\bm{x}}_{i}/\lVert{\bm{W}}_{j}{\bm{x}}_{i}\rVert_{2}, nhn_{h} values as ūĚíói,j=ūĚĎĹj‚ÄčūĚíôi{\bm{v}}_{i,j}={\bm{V}}_{j}{\bm{x}}_{i}, and nhn_{h} betas as ő≤i,j=Ōē‚Äč(ūĚĎľj‚ÄčūĚíôi)\beta_{i,j}=\phi({\bm{U}}_{j}{\bm{x}}_{i}) where ūĚĎĺj,ūĚĎĹj,ūĚĎľj{\bm{W}}_{j},{\bm{V}}_{j},{\bm{U}}_{j}, are learnable weight matrices specific to the jj-th gradient step, while Ōē\phi is either the sigmoid or 2√ó2\times the sigmoid as suggested by Grazzi et al. (2025) to increase the expressivity. Then, we compute nhn_{h} gradient descent steps using the losses ‚Ąíi,j‚Äč(ūĚĎĮ)=‚ÄĖūĚĎĮ‚ä§‚ÄčūĚíĆi,j‚ąíūĚíói,j‚ÄĖ22/2\mathcal{L}_{i,j}({\bm{H}})=\|{\bm{H}}^{\top}{\bm{k}}_{i,j}-{\bm{v}}_{i,j}\|_{2}^{2}/2, i.e. for j=1‚Äč‚Ä¶‚Äčnhj=1\dots n_{h}

where ūĚĎĮi,0=ūĚĎĮi‚ąí1{\bm{H}}_{i,0}={\bm{H}}_{i-1} and ūĚĎĮi,nh=ūĚĎĮi{\bm{H}}_{i,n_{h}}={\bm{H}}_{i}. Unrolling, we get ūĚĎĮi=ūĚĎ®‚Äč(ūĚíôi)‚ÄčūĚĎĮi‚ąí1+ūĚĎ©‚Äč(ūĚíôi){\bm{H}}_{i}={\bm{A}}({\bm{x}}_{i}){\bm{H}}_{i-1}+{\bm{B}}({\bm{x}}_{i}) with

Hence, by taking multiple gradient descent steps per token, DeltaProduct‚Äôs state-transition matrices are products of generalized Householder transformations, and by expanding such product, ūĚĎ®‚Äč(ūĚíôi){\bm{A}}({\bm{x}}_{i}) takes the form of identity plus a matrix of rank at most nhn_{h}. This formulation enables DeltaProduct to interpolate between generalized Householder (nh=1n_{h}=1 as in DeltaNet) and dense matrices, with the level of expressivity and computational complexity controlled by nhn_{h}.

An important consequence of using Householder products is that it allows us to efficiently bound the norm of ūĚĎ®‚Äč(ūĚíôi){\bm{A}}({\bm{x}}_{i}), since the norm of the product is upper bounded by the product of the norms (each ‚Č§1\leq 1), ensuring the stability of the recurrence. This bound would not be possible with a the direct formulation ūĚĎ®‚Äč(ūĚíôi)=I‚ąí‚ąĎj=1nhő≤i,j‚ÄčūĚíĆi,j‚ÄčūĚíĆi,j‚ä§{\bm{A}}({\bm{x}}_{i})=I-\sum_{j=1}^{n_{h}}\beta_{i,j}{\bm{k}}_{i,j}{\bm{k}}_{i,j}^{\top}, which would also restrict the matrix to be symmetric. Notably, for each layer, DeltaProduct uses nhn_{h} distinct key projection matrices to generate different keys ūĚíĆi,1,‚Ä¶,ūĚíĆi,nh{\bm{k}}_{i,1},\dots,{\bm{k}}_{i,n_{h}} for the same input token. This ability to generate multiple, potentially orthogonal keys is essential for enhancing the recurrence‚Äôs expressivity beyond DeltaNet. If we were to use identical keys across steps, the product of generalized Householders would collapse into a single generalized Householder transformation with a scaled ő≤\beta (see Lemma 1).

In contrast to increasing the number of gradient steps per token, the expressivity of DeltaNet (equivalent to DeltaProduct with nh=1n_{h}=1) can also be enhanced by increasing the number of layers and its theoretical limit is still unknown. In Grazzi et al. (2025, Theorem 6) it is shown that with 2 layers and the extended eigenvalue range, DeltaNet can compute addition modulo mm, which corresponds to solving the group word problem for the cyclic group ‚Ą§m\mathbb{Z}_{m}, for any m‚ąą‚Ąēm\in\mathbb{N}.

We extend Grazzi et al. (2025, Theorem 6) to prove that, under identical assumptions, DeltaNet can solve the group word problem for the dihedral group DmD_{m}, for any m‚ąą‚Ąēm\in\mathbb{N}. The dihedral group DmD_{m} represents the symmetries (both rotations and reflections) of a regular mm-sided polygon. As a notable example, D3D_{3} is isomorphic to the symmetric group S3S_{3}. The linear RNN construction used in this result can be implemented using a 2-layer DeltaNet with two heads in the first layer.

The complete proof is provided in Appendix B and uses the following construction. In the first layer, the linear RNN will compute parity for rotations and reflections separately, i.e. it will record if the number of rotations (reflections) is even or odd. The recurrent state of the second layer will have 2‚Äčm2m possible values (same as the order of DmD_{m}) and each will be decoded differently based on the parity of reflections. The parity of rotations, combined with the group element, determines which reflection matrix to use as the state transition matrix.

We evaluate DeltaProduct on a range of tasks‚ÄĒfrom state-tracking and chomsky hierarchy problems to standard language modeling‚ÄĒto assess its expressivity and efficiency. In each experiment, we vary the number of Householder transformations per token (nhn_{h}) while keeping other parameters fixed, thereby trading-off increased computational cost for enhanced expressivity. Throughout the experiments we use either the suffix [‚ąí1,1][-1,1] or [0,1][0,1] after each method, to denote the eigenvalue ranges of its state transition matrices.

subsections.title: 5.1 State-Tracking
paragraphs: We evaluate DeltaProduct‚Äôs ability to capture complex state dynamics using group word problems of increasing difficulty, specifically on the permutation groups S3S_{3}, S4S_{4}, A5A_{5}, and S5S_{5}, as implemented by Merrill et al. (2024). We train on sequences of up to 128 products of permutations/tokens in length and subsequently measure extrapolation on sequences of up to 512 tokens. Throughout, we use the extended setting, allowing eigenvalues in [‚ąí1,1][-1,1]. DeltaProduct models failed to learn even the training context-length when restricted to the standard eigenvalue range [0,1][0,1], regardless of the number of Householder transformations nhn_{h} and so we omit the results. See Section C.1 for details on the experimental setup.

Figure 2 (top row) demonstrates the benefits of increasing the number of gradient steps nhn_{h} per token for a single layer DeltaProduct. In agreement with Grazzi et al. (2025, Theorem 3), for S3S_{3}, achieving reliable performance beyond sequence lengths of 128 requires nh=2n_{h}=2, while S5S_{5} needs nh=4n_{h}=4. Unexpectedly, S4S_{4} and A5A_{5} can extrapolate robustly using only nh=2n_{h}=2, even though the aforementioned theorem would suggest 3 and 4 respectively. This is due to them being isomorphic to subgroups of the special orthogonal group SO‚Äč(3,‚ĄĚ)\mathrm{SO}(3,\mathbb{R}) (Schwarzbach, 2010, Ch. 1, Sec. 2.4), which enables each element of these groups to be mapped to a 3D rotation using just two Householder reflections (see Figure 3 for an illustration).

In Figure 2 (bottom row), we explore the limits of the expressivity of DeltaNet (i.e. nh=1n_{h}=1) with multiple layers, which is still not fully understood theoretically. Increasing the number of layers while keeping nh=1n_{h}=1 improves performance but less effectively than increasing nhn_{h}. Interestingly, DeltaNet cannot learn S4S_{4} and S5S_{5} even with 5 layers, and with two layers, it performs poorly also on S3S_{3}, even though Theorem 1 shows that it can solve it. This suggests that simply increasing the number of layers may not be sufficient to attain the improvements of DeltaProduct enabled by incorporating more Householder transformations.

To empirically validate whether DeltaProduct[‚ąí1,1]2{}_{2}[-1,1] learns to perform rotations for S4S_{4}, we analyze the estimated values of two key parameters, ő≤i,1\beta_{i,1} and ő≤i,2\beta_{i,2}, during the network‚Äôs operation. We tested this by running all possible permutations of S4S_{4} (24 in total) and recording their corresponding ő≤1\beta_{1} and ő≤2\beta_{2} values (from the first and second generalized householder in the product, respectively).

Our findings revealed that a single head learned to use both Householder transformations as reflections (where ő≤0=ő≤1=2\beta_{0}=\beta_{1}=2) to create rotation matrices (see Figure 7). This pattern becomes clear in Figure 4 (left), where nearly all beta values for this head are equal to 2. Therefore, the model learns to approximate rotations by combining two reflections, which explains why it can effectively represent S4S_{4} using just two Householder matrices.

To further understand the dimensionality of this representation, we applied Principal Component Analysis (PCA) (Pearson, 1901) to the key vectors of the attention head where beta values approached 2. The results, shown in Figure 4 (right), revealed that three principal components account for over 95% of the variance in the key space. This finding aligns with our theoretical understanding, as it suggests the model primarily operates in a three-dimensional subspace, consistent with the special orthogonal group SO‚Äč(3,‚ĄĚ)\mathrm{SO}(3,\mathbb{R}).

subsections.title: 5.2 Chomsky Hierarchy
paragraphs: We conducted experiments on selected formal language tasks originally introduced by Deletang et al. (2023). Our goal is to demonstrate the improvements in length extrapolation that can be achieved using multiple Householder matrices in the state-transition matrix compared to DeltaNet. Following Grazzi et al. (2025), we focus on three tasks: parity, modular arithmetic without brackets (both regular languages), and modular arithmetic with brackets (a context-free language). We trained DeltaProductnh{}_{n_{h}} with nh‚ąą{2,3,4}n_{h}\in\{2,3,4\} on sequences of length 3 to 40 and tested on sequences ranging from 40 to 256 to evaluate generalization to longer inputs. We compare our results against Transformer, mLSTM and sLSTM from Beck et al. (2024), Mamba (Gu & Dao, 2024), and DeltaNet (Yang et al., 2024b). For both Mamba and DeltaNet, we experiment with eigenvalue ranges restricted to [0,1][0,1] and extended to [‚ąí1,1][-1,1].

As shown in Table 1, DeltaProductnh{}_{n_{h}} with nh‚Č•2n_{h}\geq 2 demonstrates greater expressivity compared to DeltaNet and other baselines. This performance improvement is particularly pronounced when using the extended eigenvalue range [‚ąí1,1][-1,1], which aligns with the findings of Grazzi et al. (2025). Notably, we observe the most significant improvement in the modular arithmetic with brackets task, where DeltaNet[‚ąí1,1][-1,1] previously showed limitations (Grazzi et al., 2025). Across all tasks, DeltaProduct[‚ąí1,1]4{}_{4}[-1,1] achieves an average accuracy of 0.778, improving 19.14% and 8.51% over sLSTM and DeltaNet[‚ąí1,1][-1,1], respectively. Additional experimental details and hyperparameter values are provided in Appendix C.2.

subsections.title: 5.3 Language Modeling
paragraphs: We trained two model variants: DeltaProductnh‚Äč[‚ąí1,1]\text{DeltaProduct}_{n_{h}}[-1,1] and Gated DeltaProductnh‚Äč[‚ąí1,1]\text{Gated DeltaProduct}_{n_{h}}[-1,1] using the FineWeb dataset (Penedo et al., 2024) with 3535B tokens. We adopted the training hyperparameters and pipeline of Grazzi et al. (2025) (detailed in Section C.3.1). We evaluated the models using language understanding, reasoning, and retrieval benchmarks from lm-eval-harness (Gao et al., 2024), with task specifics in Section C.3.2. To assess extrapolation, we measured perplexity beyond the training context length of 2048 tokens on CodeParrot (Tunstall et al., 2022) for coding, OpenThoughts-114k-Math (Team, 2025) for math, TriviaQA (Joshi et al., 2017) for knowledge retrieval, and SlimPajama (Soboleva et al., 2023) for general language modeling.

Our experiments demonstrate that both DeltaProduct and Gated DeltaProduct on average outperform their baseline counterparts (DeltaNet‚Äč[‚ąí1,1]\text{DeltaNet}[-1,1] and Gated DeltaNet‚Äč[‚ąí1,1]\text{Gated DeltaNet}[-1,1]) across the considered language modeling benchmarks when we increase nhn_{h}, as shown in Table 2. Interestingly, DeltaProduct3‚Äč[‚ąí1,1]\text{DeltaProduct}_{3}[-1,1] achieves comparable performance to Gated DeltaNet‚Äč[‚ąí1,1]\text{Gated DeltaNet}[-1,1], despite lacking a forget gate mechanism - a component considered crucial for language modeling tasks (Hochreiter & Schmidhuber, 1997; Gu & Dao, 2024). Furthermore, our training process remained stable even as we increased the value of nhn_{h} (see Figure 8).

Remarkably, as shown in Figure 5, DeltaProduct‚Äôs length extrapolation performance increases significantly with higher nhn_{h} values, and at nh=3n_{h}=3, the performance degradation is minimal across the sequence length (see also Figure 9 for results up to 32k sequence length).

We hypothesize that DeltaProduct achieves better length extrapolation by enhancing DeltaNet‚Äôs forgetting mechanism. While DeltaNet requires nn rank-1 updates to reset its state to zero, DeltaProduct can accelerate this forgetting process by a factor of nhn_{h}. This improvement can allow DeltaProduct[‚ąí1,1]3{}_{3}[-1,1] to learn effective forgetting patterns during training without an additional scalar forget gate, unlike Gated DeltaNet. With a head embedding dimension of 128, DeltaProduct[‚ąí1,1]3{}_{3}[-1,1] can reset its state in approximately 43 tokens, making it much more efficient at handling long-range dependencies. However, our experiments show that DeltaProduct[‚ąí1,1]2{}_{2}[-1,1] still performs better with a forget gate, as demonstrated by its improved results when compared to the non-gated version (see Figure 9). To fairly compare DeltaProduct and DeltaNet, we conducted scaling experiments that accounted for DeltaProduct‚Äôs additional parameters from its extra key, value, and ő≤\beta projection layers. We varied the number of attention heads in both models. As shown in Figure 6 (top), DeltaProduct consistently achieves better performance than DeltaNet, though the perplexity difference is modest. We expanded our analysis by evaluating DeltaProduct[‚ąí1,1]2{}_{2}[-1,1] and DeltaNet across multiple benchmarks from lm-eval-harness (Gao et al., 2024). The results, shown in Figure 6 (bottom), reinforce our findings from the fineweb dataset. DeltaProduct maintains its performance advantage on both WikiText and Lambada tasks, showing improved perplexity across all model configurations. Our results align with the findings from Section 5.1 on state tracking: adding more householders proves more effective for improving length extrapolation compared to increasing the number of heads/layers.

We presented DeltaProduct, an extension of DeltaNet that uses products of Householder transformations as state-transition matrices. This approach bridges the gap between structured and dense matrices, with each recurrence step interpretable as multiple steps of gradient descent on an associative recall loss (compared to DeltaNet‚Äôs single step). The number of Householder matrices (nhn_{h}) serves as a tunable parameter balancing expressivity and computational efficiency. Our experiments demonstrate DeltaProduct‚Äôs superior performance over DeltaNet in state tracking, formal language recognition, and language modeling, with particularly strong results in length extrapolation tasks. DeltaProduct represents a promising step toward developing sequence models that are both more capable while still remaining computationally efficient.

subsections.title: C.1 State-Tracking
paragraphs: We used the experimental setup from Merrill et al. (2024) and sampled 2,000,0002{,}000{,}000 training datapoints at sequence length 128 and 500,000500{,}000 test datapoints at sequence length 512. We did not use a curriculum over sequence length during training. The models were trained using AdamW optimizer (Loshchilov & Hutter, 2019) with parameters ő≤1=0.9\beta_{1}=0.9, ő≤2=0.999\beta_{2}=0.999, and ŌĶ=10‚ąí8\epsilon=10^{-8} in PyTorch (Paszke et al., 2019). We used a learning rate of 10‚ąí310^{-3} with cosine annealing (Loshchilov & Hutter, 2017) and trained for 100 epochs with a batch size of 1024, except for the S3S_{3} models which required a batch size of 2048 for more reliable results. All models used a single-layer DeltaProduct architecture featuring 12 attention heads (more heads made the results more reliable) and a head dimension of 32. We applied a weight decay coefficient of 10‚ąí610^{-6}. The ő≤\beta values were extracted from the forward pass of the trained models using NNsight (Fiotto-Kaufman et al., 2024). We use the PCA implementation in scikit-learn (Pedregosa et al., 2011).

[Figure: Figure 7: ő≤0\beta_{0} and ő≤1\beta_{1} values across all 24 permutations in S4S_{4} in DeltaProduct[‚ąí1,1]2{}_{2}[-1,1]. We find that only head 6 (shown in Figure 4) learns to use both Householders as reflections (ő≤0‚Čą2\beta_{0}\approx 2, ő≤1‚Čą2\beta_{1}\approx 2) allowing it to learn the rotation to solve S4S_{4}.]

subsections.title: C.2 Chomsky Hierarchy
paragraphs: Here, we provide additional details on the formal language tasks and experimental protocol of Section 5.2.

Similar to Beck et al. (2024), we trained each model with sequence lengths ranging from 3 to 40 and evaluated on lengths from 40 to 256, to understand the length generalization capabilities. We take the results shown in Table 1 for Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017), mLSTM, sLSTM (Beck et al., 2024), Mamba (Gu & Dao, 2024) directly from Grazzi et al. (2025). All DeltaProduct and DeltaNet models contain 3 layers with 1 head each. We set the embedding and heads‚Äô dimensions to 128. Both models use a causal depthwise 1D convolution with a kernel size of 4 after the query/key/value projection. For modular arithmetic, we also use a gradient clipping norm of 1.0. We train each model using AdamW (Loshchilov & Hutter, 2019) using a learning rate of 5e-4, batch size of 1024, 0.1 weight decay, and a cosine annealing learning rate schedule (Loshchilov & Hutter, 2017) (minimum learning rate: 1e-6) after 10% warm-up steps. We train on the modular arithmetic and parity tasks for 100k and 20k steps in total, respectively. At each training step, we make sure to generate a valid random sample from the task at hand (see below). We repeat the runs 3 times with different seeds each, and later pick the best to report in Table 1.

In Section 5.2, we empirically evaluated three tasks‚ÄĒparity, modular arithmetic without brackets, and modular arithmetic with brackets‚ÄĒspanning different levels of the Chomsky Hierarchy. These tasks were originally introduced by Deletang et al. (2023) and later used for benchmarking xLSTM (Beck et al., 2024, Figure 4). Below, we provide details for each task, where |ő£||\Sigma| denotes the vocabulary size and A‚Äčc‚Äčcr‚Äča‚Äčn‚ÄčdAcc_{rand} represents the accuracy of random guessing:

‚ÄĘ Parity (|ő£|=2|\Sigma|=2, A‚Äčc‚Äčcr‚Äča‚Äčn‚Äčd=0.5Acc_{rand}=0.5). Given a binary sequence ūĚíô=x1‚Äč‚Ä¶‚Äčxt‚ąą{0,1}t{\bm{x}}=x_{1}\dots x_{t}\in\{0,1\}^{t}, the parity label yt‚ąą{0,1}y_{t}\in\{0,1\} is 1 if the total number of ones in the sequence is odd, and 0 otherwise. This task is equivalent to computing the sum of all previous values modulo 2, i.e., yt=(‚ąĎi=1txi)mod2y_{t}=(\sum_{i=1}^{t}x_{i})\bmod 2.

‚ÄĘ Modular Arithmetic without Brackets (|ő£|=10|\Sigma|=10, A‚Äčc‚Äčcr‚Äča‚Äčn‚Äčd=1/5Acc_{rand}=1/5). Given a set of special tokens ő£s={+,‚ąí,‚ąó,=,[ūĚôŅūĚôįūĚô≥]}\Sigma_{s}=\{\mathtt{+,-,*,=,[PAD]}\} and a modulus m‚Č•1m\geq 1, we define ő£=ő£s‚ą™{0,‚Ä¶,m‚ąí1}\Sigma=\Sigma_{s}\cup\{0,\dots,m-1\}. The label yty_{t} corresponds to the result of evaluating the arithmetic operations in the sequence ūĚíô=x1,‚Ä¶,xt{\bm{x}}=x_{1},\dots,x_{t}, computed modulo mm. In our experiments, we set m=5m=5. An example is:

‚ÄĘ Modular Arithmetic with Brackets (|ő£|=12|\Sigma|=12, A‚Äčc‚Äčcr‚Äča‚Äčn‚Äčd=1/5Acc_{rand}=1/5). This task follows the same definition as modular arithmetic without brackets but includes an extended set of special tokens, ő£s={+,‚ąí,‚ąó,=,),(,[ūĚôŅūĚôįūĚô≥]}\Sigma_{s}=\{\mathtt{+,-,*,=,),(,[PAD]}\}, allowing for nested expressions. Again, we set m=5m=5. An example sequence is:

Parity (|ő£|=2|\Sigma|=2, A‚Äčc‚Äčcr‚Äča‚Äčn‚Äčd=0.5Acc_{rand}=0.5). Given a binary sequence ūĚíô=x1‚Äč‚Ä¶‚Äčxt‚ąą{0,1}t{\bm{x}}=x_{1}\dots x_{t}\in\{0,1\}^{t}, the parity label yt‚ąą{0,1}y_{t}\in\{0,1\} is 1 if the total number of ones in the sequence is odd, and 0 otherwise. This task is equivalent to computing the sum of all previous values modulo 2, i.e., yt=(‚ąĎi=1txi)mod2y_{t}=(\sum_{i=1}^{t}x_{i})\bmod 2.

Modular Arithmetic without Brackets (|ő£|=10|\Sigma|=10, A‚Äčc‚Äčcr‚Äča‚Äčn‚Äčd=1/5Acc_{rand}=1/5). Given a set of special tokens ő£s={+,‚ąí,‚ąó,=,[ūĚôŅūĚôįūĚô≥]}\Sigma_{s}=\{\mathtt{+,-,*,=,[PAD]}\} and a modulus m‚Č•1m\geq 1, we define ő£=ő£s‚ą™{0,‚Ä¶,m‚ąí1}\Sigma=\Sigma_{s}\cup\{0,\dots,m-1\}. The label yty_{t} corresponds to the result of evaluating the arithmetic operations in the sequence ūĚíô=x1,‚Ä¶,xt{\bm{x}}=x_{1},\dots,x_{t}, computed modulo mm. In our experiments, we set m=5m=5. An example is:

Modular Arithmetic with Brackets (|ő£|=12|\Sigma|=12, A‚Äčc‚Äčcr‚Äča‚Äčn‚Äčd=1/5Acc_{rand}=1/5). This task follows the same definition as modular arithmetic without brackets but includes an extended set of special tokens, ő£s={+,‚ąí,‚ąó,=,),(,[ūĚôŅūĚôįūĚô≥]}\Sigma_{s}=\{\mathtt{+,-,*,=,),(,[PAD]}\}, allowing for nested expressions. Again, we set m=5m=5. An example sequence is:

subsections.title: C.3 Language Modeling
paragraphs: We follow the same basic training setup as in (Grazzi et al., 2025). We use the training pipeline flame from the flash-linear-attention (Yang & Zhang, 2024) repository. All of our models are trained on NVIDIA L40s or NVIDIA A100 40GB GPUs. We used 16 to 32 GPUs at a time to train one model, in either a 2 or 4 node setup, depending on resource availability. We used DeepSpeed with ZeRO-2 (Rajbhandari et al., 2020) for distributed training. All models were trained for 66 75866\,758 steps with a global batch size of 524 288524\,288, a learning rate of 3e-4, and a training context length of 2 0482\,048 tokens. We used two steps of gradient accumulation in the 16 GPU setup. We optimized the models with AdamW (Loshchilov & Hutter, 2019) (0.010.01 weight decay) and used cosine annealing (Loshchilov & Hutter, 2017) for the learning rate schedule with linear warm up for 512 steps.
We use the lm-eval-harness benchmark (Gao et al., 2024) to assess model performance. Following Yang et al. (2024b), the evaluation encompasses multiple task categories:

The evaluation includes LAMBADA (LMB) (Paperno et al., 2016) for testing text comprehension, PIQA (Bisk et al., 2020) for physical reasoning assessment, HellaSwag (Hella.) (Zellers et al., 2019) for situational understanding, and Winogrande (Wino.) (Sakaguchi et al., 2021) for commonsense reasoning evaluation.
The ARC dataset provides two distinct testing sets: ARC-easy (ARC-e) and ARC-challenge (ARC-c) (Clark et al., 2018), measuring varying levels of scientific knowledge comprehension.
The evaluation incorporates recall-intensive assessments through FDA (Arora et al., 2023), SWDE (Lockard et al., 2019), and SQUAD (Rajpurkar et al., 2018).

[Figure: Figure 8: Training loss curves of DeltaProductnh‚Äč[‚ąí1,1]\text{DeltaProduct}_{n_{h}}[-1,1]. The curves demonstrate stable training behavior as nhn_{h} increases, with higher values of nhn_{h} consistently yielding lower losses throughout training and convergence. While the absolute differences in loss between different nhn_{h} values are relatively small, they correspond to significant differences in length extrapolation performance.]
